creator: fbsd@www.zefox.net
text:
(In reply to Sulev-Madis Silber from comment #6)

I've never done any explicit testing, just passive observation.

The usual scenario is to have one Pi holding a usb-serial adapter
with its serial end connected to the console of a second Pi. I ssh
into the first Pi as a regular user, su to root and start a tip/cu
session, which brings up the console login on the second Pi. At that
point I log in on the second Pi's console, which starts a shell in 
the usual fashion. Stray characters are not seen at this stage.

Next, the serial connection to the second Pi disconnects for some reason,
sometimes being deliberately broken by me using ~~., sometimes by by
rebooting the second Pi, occasionally by dropping the ssh connection
on the first Pi. 

At this point I either restart the tip/cu session or restart the ssh
on the first Pi and start over from the beginning. Once tip comes up,
hitting the enter key often provokes an error response from the second
Pi, whose shell is still running. Hitting the up-arrow key to recall past
commands on the second Pi's shell frequently shows garbage characters in
the shell command history, implying they originated on the first Pi's 
Tx wires. 

Thus it looked as if some process on the first Pi injected characters into
the usb-serial adapter's Tx stream while cu/tip was not in control of the
adapter. 

The usual cu command used was 
cu -l cuaU0 -s 115200

The usual tip command used was 
tip ucom
where /etc/remote contains
....
ucom:dv=/dev/cuaU0:br#115200:pa=none:

It appears that you are observing characters injected into the Rx line of
the usb-serial adapter corrupted and echoed back to Tx, where they enter
the command history during the time the usb-serial adapter isn't under 
control of cu. 

I've always assumed that with the usb-serial adapter un-activated at the
usb end echo would be off by default. Maybe not, or at least not always.

One recurrent problem I've observed is that during reboot of the serial-end
FreeBSD host the boot process will sometimes skip the loader boot countdown,
dropping into the OK prompt. Hitting the enter key causes an error response,
but typing "boot" results in boot proceeding normally. However, this often
happens when the usb-serial adapter is under active control of cu/tip at the
usb end. That isn't consistent with the usb-serial adapter being idle.

Perhaps the two phenomena are related, but not very directly.

Thanks for writing!

bob prohaska
